coredata.dc:description: Ellagic acid (EA), a natural and environmentally friendly polyphenol, positively impacts human health and is widely used in the food and medical industries. It features a well-organized planar structure with an extensive sp2 carbon-conjugated area and four phenolic hydroxyl groups, making it favorable for light energy absorption and fluorescent emission. This positions EA as a promising candidate for advanced fluorescence applications. To date, the fluorescence properties of EA have not been thoroughly investigated. In this research, we comprehensively examined the fluorescence properties of EA in various solvents—including alkaline aqueous, alcoholic, nitrogenous, other organic solvents, and polyethylene glycols (PEGs)—using transient/steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy, ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, and density functional theory calculations. Additionally, the relationship between structural changes of EA in these solvents and its fluorescence properties was deeply investigated. It was discovered that general solvation effects (solvent polarity effects), hydrogen bonding effects, and viscosity effects are the main ways that solvents alter the optical characteristics of EA. EA exhibits outstanding fluorescence characteristics in PEG400, creating a bright green fluorescence with the highest Stokes shift at 170 nm among the 32 organic solvents and alkaline aqueous solutions examined. This study is both foundational and essential, setting the stage for advancing the research on EA-based fluorescent materials in future endeavors.

head.ce:title: A green plant-derived fluorescent polyphenol: Investigating the fluorescence properties of ellagic acid in diverse solvents

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ellagic acid (EA), a natural and environmentally friendly polyphenol, positively impacts human health and is widely used in the food and medical industries. It features a well-organized planar structure with an extensive sp2 carbon-conjugated area and four phenolic hydroxyl groups, making it favorable for light energy absorption and fluorescent emission. This positions EA as a promising candidate for advanced fluorescence applications. To date, the fluorescence properties of EA have not been thoroughly investigated. In this research, we comprehensively examined the fluorescence properties of EA in various solvents—including alkaline aqueous, alcoholic, nitrogenous, other organic solvents, and polyethylene glycols (PEGs)—using transient/steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy, ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, and density functional theory calculations. Additionally, the relationship between structural changes of EA in these solvents and its fluorescence properties was deeply investigated. It was discovered that general solvation effects (solvent polarity effects), hydrogen bonding effects, and viscosity effects are the main ways that solvents alter the optical characteristics of EA. EA exhibits outstanding fluorescence characteristics in PEG400, creating a bright green fluorescence with the highest Stokes shift at 170 nm among the 32 organic solvents and alkaline aqueous solutions examined. This study is both foundational and essential, setting the stage for advancing the research on EA-based fluorescent materials in future endeavors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ellagic acid (EA), a natural polyphenolic compound, is prevalent in various plants, including raspberries, blackberries, blueberries, pomegranates, and pineapples, among others [1–3]. It has been shown that in many plant extracts, the free form of EA contains more than 50 % and even up to 70 % of the total EA content [4]. The multifaceted applications of this natural polyphenol in biomedicine, environmental monitoring, and food have garnered considerable attention. Extensive research, both domestically and internationally, has underscored the positive impact of EA on human health. Its bioactive properties are diverse, encompassing enhanced anti-inflammatory [5,6], antioxidant [7], anti-apoptotic [8], antiviral [9,10], anti-tumour [11,12], bacteriostatic [13,14], scavenging of free radicals [15,16], glucose-lowering [17], lipid-lowering [18], hepatoprotecting [19], neuroprotecting [20], and immunomodulating [21,22], among other benefits [23]. Beyond its biomedical uses, some scholars have noted the scientific relevance of EA in the field of advanced materials [24,25]. This includes its application in copolymers [26,27], chelating agents [28], ion-exchange resins [29,30], electrochemical devices [31–33], and organic electronics [34].

Additionally, EA boasts a captivating molecular architecture ideal for light emission. It features a well-organized planar structure alongside an extensive sp2 carbon-conjugated area (see Fig. 1 ). This structure bears a striking resemblance to that of various polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, including pyrene, fluorene, and coumarin [35–37]. Furthermore, EA is characterised by the presence of four phenolic hydroxyl groups and two lactones, endowing EA with the capacity to accept electrons from a variety of substrates. This unique composition facilitates its involvement in antioxidant redox reactions [3,37,38]. The molecular structure of EA underscores its remarkable potential for fluorescence properties. This feature facilitates its diverse applications in the realms of fluorescence probes [39–43], bio-imaging, and biosensors [44–47], showcasing its versatility and significance in these advanced fields.

Currently, numerous researchers are conducting extensive studies on the application of EA for the construction of fluorescence systems, particularly focusing on combining EA with other elements to achieve target fluorescence [48–55]. However, studies focusing on EA as the main component in fluorescence systems are still in their infancy. This is largely due to the nascent stage of research on the fluorescence behaviours and properties of EA molecules, which results in a limited understanding of their potential in fluorescence. Therefore, conducting thorough investigations into the fluorescence properties of EA molecules is essential, as it will open new avenues for research. Moreover, the ability to modulate fluorescence properties through structural adjustments and chemical modifications of EA molecules promises further opportunities in the design and creation of innovative fluorescence materials. It should be noted that free EA is insoluble in water and is only soluble in some alcohols and other organic solvents [56]. The limited solubility of EA can be ascribed to its pronounced crystallinity (no melting point observed due to decomposition at approximately 360 °C); this high crystallinity stems from the planar and symmetric structure of EA, coupled with the extensive network of hydrogen bonds (H-bonds) formed between EA molecules [57]. Meanwhile, the solubility of EA is closely linked to the aggregation state of its molecules within the solvent. This state significantly influences molecular mobility following the absorption of energy from excitation light. The analysis underscores the importance of selecting an appropriate solvent for EA, as well as understanding how solvation influences its fluorescence characteristics. This crucial step is not only foundational but also essential in propelling forward the investigation of EA-based fluorescence materials in forthcoming research endeavors.

Materials. Ellagic acid (98 %) was purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dimethyl Sulfoxide (DMSO) was provided by Titan Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Triethanolamine and ethanolamine were supplied by Fuyu Fine Chemical Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Anhydrous methanol, anhydrous ethanol, 1-propanol, 1-butanol, 1-pentanol, 1-octanol, ethylene glycol, 1,2-propanediol, glycerol, 1,4-butanediol, acetonitrile, tetrahydrofuran, 1,4-dioxane, acetone, n-pentyl acetate, formic acid, glacial acetic acid and methylene chloride were provided by Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Aniline, N,N-Dimethylformamide, PEG200, PEG300, PEG400, PEG600, PEG800, PEG1000, PEG4000, N,N-Dimethylacetamide, and N-Methylpyrrolidone were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Information on the chemicals used in this study is detailed in Table S1.

Solubility of EA in the aqueous. 30 mg EA was added to 100 mL water and then stirred continuously in a thermostatic water bath at 25 °C. The pH of the solutions was adjusted to 7–14 with 0.01 M NaOH aqueous solution, and 5 mL of samples were taken at pH = 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, and 14 respectively for backup, while the dissolution corresponding to each pH was observed.

Solubility of EA in organic phase. In this study, the static equilibrium method was used to determine the solubility of EA in a variety of different solvents. The temperature and pressure of the solution system were kept constant during the dissolution process, and the mass of EA dissolved in 100 g of solvent at saturation was calculated as the final solubility value. The procedure was carried out as follows: an excess of EA weighted M1 was added to a conical flask containing 30 g of solvent, and then the sealed conical flask was placed in a water bath stirrer and stirred at a constant temperature (25 °C) for 48 h to fully dissolve the EA. After dissolution, the solution was then pump-filtered through a 0.45 μm filter membrane. The undissolved EA adhering to the surface of the filter membrane was then placed in a Petri dish. Finally, the Petri dish was placed in a vacuum-drying oven for 48 h to obtain a completely dried filter membrane. The operating conditions of the vacuum drying oven were set at 60 °C and 20 kPa. During the process, the membrane was weighed with an analytical balance, and M2 marked the weight of the membrane before use, while M3 marked the mass of the membrane after drying. The total mass dissolved can be obtained by subtraction. In addition, during the drying process, the mass of the filter membrane was measured every 6 h until the difference was less than 0.0005 g. To ensure the accuracy and reliability of the data, three parallel experimental measurements were carried out for each data point, and the average value was taken as the final experimental value of solubility.

Fluorescence Detection. Fluorescence detection was carried out using a dark box UV analyser (ZF-20A, Shanghai Suke Industrial Co., Ltd.). The solution with a concentration of 10 ppm was placed in a 10 mm*10 mm quartz fluorescence cuvette and put into the UV analyser. The 365 nm OLED light source was selected for fluorescence testing.

Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra and fluorescence emission spectra. Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra or fluorescence emission spectra were scanned using a transient/steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, FS5). Samples with a concentration of 1–100 ppm were placed in a cuvette with excitation and emission slit wavelengths of 1–20 nm, excitation peaks were scanned in the range of 300 nm–450 nm, and emission peaks were scanned in the range of 380 nm–700 nm. Specific values were determined based on reagent testing.

Fluorescence lifetime (τ) Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a transient/steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, FS5). According to the principle of the "Single Photon Counting (SPC) method", after the sample was excited by a pulsed light source, the sample emitted a fluorescence photon signal. After each pulse, only the time (t) of a specific wavelength of a single photon appearing was recorded. After multiple counts, the probability distribution of the appearance of fluorescence photons, P(t), was measured. A sample with a concentration of 50 ppm was placed in a cuvette with excitation and emission slit wavelengths of 0.1 nm and 20 nm, respectively, and a 380 nm excitation lamp was used for fluorescence testing. The fluorescence lifetime was calculated and fitted to the model [58,59] as follows:

Fluorescence quantum yield (QY). The fluorescence quantum yield was determined by comparing the fluorescence intensity integral (i.e., the corrected area of the fluorescence spectrum) and absorbance of the fluorescence substance to be tested and a reference substance with a known quantum yield under the same excitation conditions. The QY value of EA in each type of solvent was calculated according to the following formula [58,59]:
QY EA

where the subscripts EA and R denote the unknown to be tested (EA) and the reference standard, respectively. QY was the fluorescence quantum yield, I is the fluorescence peak area, A was the absorbance (Absorbance is controlled below 0.1 for reliability of measurements during testing), and η refers to the refractive index of the solvent (due to the very low concentration of the unknown to be tested and the reference standard in the experiment, the effect of solute can be ignored and considered as consistent). In this study, the quantum yield (QY) of quinine sulphate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 was used as a reference substance, and the QY value of quinine sulphate was determined to be 0.54 at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm [58,59].

Ultraviolet–visible light (UV–Vis) Absorption spectra. The EA solutions of different solvents were taken and diluted to the appropriate concentration with the corresponding solvents, and the UV–Vis absorption spectra were determined using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (GENESYS 50, Thermo Scientific, China).

Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) Spectroscopy . Sample preparation: 90 mg of EA was taken into 300 mL of water and stirred continuously in a thermostatic water bath at 25 °C. Then pH = 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14 was adjusted with 0.1 M NaOH aqueous solution. For each pH value, 20 mL of samples were taken into a lyophiliser for vacuum freeze-drying, and then the lyophilised samples were subjected to FTIR. FTIR (TENSON 27, Bruker, Germany) was used to determine the characteristic absorption peaks of EA at different pH values and solvents. The scanning range of FTIR was 4000 cm−1-400 cm−1, and 32 scans were accumulated.

Density Functional Theory (DFT) Calculations. All DFT calculations were performed using the Gaussian 16 procedure. The geometrical configurations were optimised by means of B3LYP floods and 6-31G(d) basis sets. HOMO and LUMO were calculated using the B3LYP method on the same basis set.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solubility and fluorescence properties of EA in the aqueous

ce:section.ce:para: In Table 1 , EA displays gradual dissolution with increasing amounts of sodium hydroxide. EA remained undissolved when the solution's pH was below 9. For pH values ≥ 9, EA gradually dissolved, causing the solution to darken as alkalinity increased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties of EA in alkali aqueous
ce:section.ce:para: The LUV colour space, also referred to as CIE (Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage) 1976, is a three-dimensional CIE chromaticity diagram with nominal values (L*,u*,v*), where u* and v* stand for chromaticity and L* for the brightness of an object. The CIE chromatograms and CIE coordinates of alkali-soluble EA at various pH levels are displayed in Fig. 2 a and Table S2. The luminous colors of alkali-soluble EA are mathematically depicted in graphs. When seen in conjunction with Table 1's fluorescence plots under 365 nm UV irradiation, it is clear that alkali-soluble EA fluoresces in the blue and cyan light areas.

An analysis of the fluorescence characteristics of EA was conducted utilizing a transient/steady-state fluorescence spectrometer. Initially, the most effective excitation wavelength by conducting a scan of the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2b and Fig. S1) of EA (25 ppm) solutions within the pH range of 9.0–14.0 was identified. Clearly, the excitation wavelengths of EA solutions varied with pH; however, the emission wavelength remained constant across different excitation wavelengths, in accordance with Kasha's rule. The fluorescence emission spectra of EA in aqueous solutions with varying pH values were obtained by scanning under the identified optimal excitation wavelength conditions (Fig. 2c). The specific values outlining the fluorescence properties of EA under different pH conditions were detailed in Table 2 . At pH = 9, the emission peak was located at 445 nm. When the pH increased to 10, 11, and 12, the EA emission peak turned to 480 nm. The Stokes shift was also enlarged by nearly two times from 85 nm to 160 nm. And the fluorescence intensity was the strongest at pH = 11, reaching the maximum value of 2.33 × 105. This demonstrates that EA exists in a deprotonated state in alkaline solutions with a pH below 12.4, and the extent of deprotonation increases as the alkalinity of the solution rises. The presence of a lone pair of electrons on the oxygen atom of the phenolic hydroxyl group facilitates an intramolecular charge transfer (ICT). More specifically, the lone electron pair in the p orbitals was conjugated with the extensive π bond, creating a p-π conjugation [60]. This interaction expanded the conjugation within the EA molecule, leading to a red shift in the emission peaks. Additionally, it caused an increase in the Stokes shift and enhanced the fluorescence intensity. When pH > 12, the emission peak of the EA solution returned to around 440 nm, with a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity and a decrease in Stokes shift. This observation could be attributed to the findings of Miki Hasegawa et al., in 2003 [61], which revealed that when the pH exceeds 12.4, the lactone ring of the double deprotonated product opens to form carboxylic derivatives. The new molecular configuration intensifies the stretching and bending vibrations of the C–O and CO bonds, increasing the likelihood of non-radiative transitions. This led to a blue shift in the emission peaks and a reduction in fluorescence intensity. In summary, EA can exhibit emission bands at different pH levels: 445 nm for its singly deprotonated form (pH < 10), 480 nm for its doubly deprotonated form (10 ≤ pH ≤ 12), and 440 nm when the lactone bond breaks (pH > 12). The molecular structures of the various states of alkali-soluble EA are shown in Fig. S7.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is defined as the ratio of emitted fluorescence photons to absorbed excitation light photons, serving as an indicator of a sample's luminescence efficiency. The magnitude of the fluorescence QY is determined by the balance between fluorescence emission and non-radiative decay processes, which is primarily influenced by the molecular structure and properties of the fluorescent materials. The regular planar conformation of EA met this requirement. However, the quantum yield (QY) of alkali-soluble EA was not particularly high, as shown in Table 2. At pH 9, the QY reaches its maximum of 32.54 % under alkaline conditions. This indicated that the deprotonation of EA's phenolic hydroxyl groups and the opening of the lactone ring disrupt the molecule's rigid planar conformation, resulting in a lower fluorescence quantum yield.

The fluorescence lifetime is the duration that a fluorescent molecule remains in its excited state. This duration was closely tied to the molecular structure of the fluorescent molecule, as well as the polarity and viscosity of the solvent, among other factors. Theories related to excited state lifetimes suggest that any process competing with spontaneous emission can shorten this lifetime. Extensive empirical and theoretical research conducted by numerous scientists has confirmed that ICT significantly influences the τ value (fluorescence lifetime) [62,63]. Therefore, the ICT effect of the lone pair of electrons on the phenolic hydroxyl oxygen in alkali-soluble EA significantly reduces its fluorescence lifetime, consistent with experimental observations. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetimes of alkali-soluble EA are notably shorter, ranging from 2 to 5 ns, as detailed in Table 2.

The fluorescence emission spectra of alkali-soluble EA at various concentrations, excited at the optimal wavelength, are depicted in Fig. 3 . The fluorescence intensity of alkali-soluble EA initially increased and then decreased with rising concentration, displaying a characteristic aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect. This phenomenon occurs as the stable planar structure of EA hinders intramolecular movement in its dispersed state. More specifically, the absorbed energy was emitted as fluorescence radiation, leading to pronounced fluorescence at lower concentrations. On the other hand, as the concentration increased, the EA molecules aggregated in the solution due to π-π stacking. This led to energy transfer between excited high-energy molecules and unexcited low-energy molecules that were in close proximity. Consequently, energy was dissipated without the need for a radiative leap, resulting in a gradual decrease in luminescence until it eventually vanishes. This phenomenon represented a considerable obstacle in the practical application of traditional colour-emitting groups [64]. Therefore, mitigating the ACQ of EA through various physical, chemical, and engineering strategies to enable robust fluorescence emission, even in aggregated states, holds substantial theoretical and practical significance [65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis absorption spectra and FTIR spectra of alkali-soluble EA
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectra of pure EA and alkalized EA were depicted in Fig. 5b and c. In Fig. 5b, the characteristic absorption peaks of EA were clearly revealed: the sharp peak at 3472 cm−1 was a stretching vibration absorption peak of the free phenolic hydroxyl group on EA; the broad peak at 3152 cm−1 was the O–H stretching vibration absorption peak of intermolecular hydrogen bonding; the broad peak at 1719 cm−1 is a CO stretching vibration absorption peak of lactone; the CC backbone vibration absorption peaks at 1618, 1585, 1506 and 1448 cm−1 respectively, were belonged to benzene rings; the C–O stretching vibrational absorption peaks at 1109 and 1042 cm−1 were belonged to the lactone; C–H out-of-plane bending vibrational absorption peak of benzene ring located at 757 cm−1 [67,68]. In comparison with Fig. 5c, it was found that the characteristic absorption peak of the free phenolic hydroxyl group of EA disappeared in an alkaline solution. Furthermore, the CO absorption peak of the lactone at 1719 cm−1 underwent a gradual red-shift with pH increasing, and the C–O absorption peaks of the lactone at 1109 and 1042 cm−1 disappeared after pH ≥ 12. This change confirmed the breakage of the lactone bond and deprotonation of the phenolic hydroxyl group of EA in the alkaline aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The solubility and fluorescence properties of EA in the organic solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The solubility of EA in four common types of organic solvents: alcohols, polyethers, nitrogenous solvent, and others, were determined. The final solubility value of EA was determined by measuring the specific mass dissolved in 100 g of solvent at saturation. We precisely depicted the fluorescence hues of EA in various solvents using the CIE1976 chromaticity diagram and documented the CIE coordinate values in Table S2. Additionally, we conducted 3D fluorescence spectral scans of EA in different solvents using a transient/steady-state fluorescence spectrometer to ascertain its optimal excitation wavelength. The 3D fluorescence spectra of EA in organic solvents such as mono alcohols, polyols, polyethers, nitrogenous solvents and others are shown in Figs. S3–S6 and Fig. 9. The succeeding fluorescence characteristics under the respective optimal excitation wavelength conditions, along with UV–Vis spectroscopic analyses, was subsequently investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The solubility and fluorescence properties of EA in alcoholic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: The solubility of EA in alcoholic organic solvents is presented in Table 3 . The solubility of EA in these solvents is generally low, with the highest solubility observed in propanol at just 0.1259 g/100 g. Nonetheless, solutions of EA in low concentrations of alcohol solvents exhibit strong blue fluorescence. According to the 3D fluorescence spectra shown in Figs. S3 and S4, the optimal excitation wavelengths for the EA/alcohol solvent mixtures are both 350 nm. Additionally, the relationship between excitation and emission wavelengths in these solutions adheres to the Kasha rule. The fluorescence emission spectra of EA in alcoholic solvents were recorded by scanning at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm (Fig. 6 a and c), and the fluorescence colors were illustrated by CIE chromaticity diagrams (Fig. 7 ). The specific values of the measured fluorescence properties are listed in Table 4 . The emission wavelength of EA in methanol alcohols was consistently at 403 nm with a Stokes shift of 53 nm. In addition, a linear monotonic increase in the fluorescence intensity of the EA solution can easily be observed with the growth of the alkyl chain of the monohydric alcohol solvent. This result is attributed to the hydrogen bonding involving the lone-pair electrons of the electron-donor substituent –OH on the aromatic ring of EA in alcohol solvents. This interaction serves as a non-radiative jump channel, which leads to a reduction in both the fluorescence quantum yield and the fluorescence intensity [69]. It also induces a blue shift in the emission wavelength. Among the six monohydric alcohol solvents from methanol to octanol, the dielectric constant ε (Table S1) of the solvent decreases, and the hydrogen bonding between the solvent and the EA molecules weakens, leading to an increase in the fluorescence intensity [70–72].

In polyol solvents, the emission wavelength of the EA solution was red-shifted to 407 nm (Fig. 6c). In contrast to mono alcohol solvents, the fluorescence intensity exhibited a non-linear correlation with the solvent's dielectric constant ε. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of the EA in polyol solvents was found to be linearly correlated with that of the solvent. The viscosities of ethylene glycol, 1,2-propanediol, 1,4-butanediol (1,4-BDO), and propanetriol at room temperature were 19.9 mPa s, 56 mPa s, 260 mPa s, and 1480 mPa s, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of the EA/polyol solution increased with the rise of the solvent viscosity. In the comparatively low viscosity ethylene glycol, the fluorescence intensity of EA was 1.41 × 104, and then jumped to 2.09 × 105 in the ultra-high viscosity propanetriol, an almost 15-fold increase. It is demonstrated that the viscosity effect dominates in high-viscosity solvents. It affects the change in fluorescence through the viscous resistance of the solvent molecules to the rotation of the moiety. Viscosity increases cause the resistance to rotation to rise, which prevents non-radiative leaps and raises the intensity of the fluorescence [73,74]. The fluorescence emission spectra of different concentrations of EA/1,4-BDO solutions at optimum excitation wavelengths demonstrate a similar ACQ phenomenon. As the concentration of EA grew from 1 ppm to 100 ppm, the intensity of its fluorescence showed a trend of increasing and then dropping. The highest fluorescence intensity, 1.80 × 105, was seen at 50 ppm.

The position of the fluorescence emission peaks in EA alcohol solvents was noted to undergo a red shift from 403 nm in monohydric alcohols to 407 nm in diols, as the polarity of the solvents increased (see Fig. 6a and c). Due to the significantly higher polarity of diol solvents compared to mono alcohol solvents, EA exhibits a larger molecular dipole moment in the diol solvent. This results in greater stabilisation energy, which in turn causes the fluorescence emission peaks to be red-shifted [75]. The UV–Vis spectra of EA in alcoholic solutions (Fig. 6e and f) displayed a consistent variation trend. In monohydric alcohols, the short-wavelength π-π* absorption peaks of EA were situated between 253 nm and 256 nm, whereas in ethylene glycol and propylene glycol, these peaks were red-shifted to 260 nm and 258 nm, respectively. For the π-π* absorption peaks of EA, the π* antibonding orbitals are more polar than the bonding orbitals of the ground state π, leading to higher solvent stabilisation energies and larger decreases in energy levels. As the polarity of the solvent increases, the π-π* absorption peaks experience a redshift. It is important to note that the solvents 1,4-BDO and glycerol, due to their excessive viscosity, considerably diminish the influence of polarity, resulting in more complex alterations in the absorption spectra [75,76].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The solubility and fluorescence properties of EA in nitrogenous solvents
ce:section.ce:para: According to the aforementioned theory, the high viscosity of the TEA solvent caused the ground state molecules of EA to adopt two distinct conformations, which subsequently affected the vibrational relaxation process. Consequently, as the excitation wavelength shifted towards the red, a corresponding red shift in the emission wavelength was observed. Additionally, Fig. 10 b displayed the fluorescence emission spectra of various concentrations of EA of NMP solutions at the optimal excitation wavelength, demonstrating the same ACQ phenomenon. The fluorescence intensity of EA initially increases and then decreases with rising concentration. The fluorescence intensity was strongest at 20 ppm with a value of 3.33 × 105. It was observed in the absorption spectra of EA in nitrogenous solvents (Fig. 9c) that the short-wavelength band π-π* jump absorption peak of EA was significantly red-shifted from 258 nm to 270–285 nm compared to that in alcohol solvents. This phenomenon occurs because polar solvents favor increased stabilisation of the charge-dispersed system, reducing the energies of both the excited and ground states, albeit to different extents. Typically, the reduction in the excited state is greater than that in the ground state. As a result, as the polarity of the solvent increases, the energy required for jump absorption decreases, causing the absorption band to shift towards longer wavelengths [75]. The π-π* absorption jump is red-shifted due to the higher polarity of these four nitrogen-containing solvents compared to alcohol solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The solubility and optical properties of EA in other organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: The solubility of EA in other organic solvents is presented in Table 7 . EA demonstrated higher solubility in DMSO and THF than others. Additionally, among the solvents, EA exhibits fluorescence properties in three organic solvents, namely DMSO, THF, and acetone. The optimal excitation wavelengths for EA in these three solvents were determined to be 380 nm, 350 nm, and 340 nm, respectively, through 3D fluorescence spectroscopy scanning maps (Fig. S6). The fluorescence emission spectra of EA in nitrogenous solvents were obtained by scanning under optimal excitation conditions (Fig. 10a). The specific fluorescence properties are listed in Table 8 . The emission wavelength of EA was 396 nm in both THF and acetone, and the fluorescence intensity was the strongest among all tested solvents. The fluorescence intensity was 8.46 × 105 and 5.23 × 105 at excitation and emission slit wavelengths of 1.5 nm and 1.6 nm, respectively. The rest of the solutions were tested at slit wavelengths greater than 2 nm, but their fluorescence intensity values were lower than those of these two solutions. Due to the lower polarity of these two solvents, they are less affected by hydrogen bonding effects, leading to an increase in fluorescence intensity. In DMSO, the emission wavelength was red-shifted to 515 nm with a Stokes shift of 135 nm. The hyperpolar nature of DMSO increased dipole-dipole interactions between the EA molecule and the solvent molecule. Furthermore, DMSO served as an excellent hydrogen-bond acceptor solvent, making it highly prone to accepting H protons from the EA molecule. The lone pair of electrons conjugating with the aromatic ring of the EA molecule resulted in a significant redshift of the emission wavelength. Furthermore, as previously stated, an increase in solvent polarity will result in a reduction in the molecular leapfrog absorption energy, causing a shift in the absorption band towards the long-wave region. In comparison to alcohol solvents, the solvent polarity of DMSO is considerably stronger. Consequently, the π-π* leap absorption peak of EA will be red-shifted. The results of the UV–Vis spectrum in Fig. 10c are consistent with this conclusion, where the absorption peak is red-shifted from 258 nm in alcohol solvents to 265 nm.

Additionally, the fluorescence of EA DMSO solutions at different concentrations exhibited the ACQ phenomenon. Like other solutions, the fluorescence of EA/DMSO solutions exhibited the ACQ phenomenon. The fluorescence intensity of EA displayed a pattern of being increasd and then decreasd with rising concentration. At just 2 ppm, the fluorescence intensity peaked at 4.08 × 105. Additionally, at concentrations of 20 ppm or higher, the emission peak shifted from 515 nm to 405 nm. This blue shift was due to the π-π stacking among a large number of EA molecules, leading to energy dissipation and a shift of the emission peak towards shorter wavelengths. Such a blue shift in the emission peak is a typical occurrence in ACQ scenarios.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The solubility and fluorescence properties of EA in PEGs
ce:section.ce:para: PEG is a non-toxic polymer and is widely used in a variety of pharmaceutical preparations. EA has good solubility in PEGs and can be directly applied to coat the surface of human skin (Fig. 11 e). Table 9 shows the solubility of EA in PEGs. Among all the solvents, the solubility of EA in PEGs was the highest. Due to the viscosity effect, the viscosity of the solvent increased as the molecular weight of the solvent increased, leading to a gradual decrease in EA solubility. In the 3D fluorescence spectra of EA in PEGs, as shown in Fig. S5, the optimal excitation wavelength was found to be 350 nm, which is consistent with that of the EA in alcoholic solution. In PEG 200–400, the excitation wavelength-emission wavelength relationship also followed the Kasha rule. To our surprise, a PEG600 solution exhibited a dual-emission fluorescence, contrary to the Kasha rule.

Using a 350 nm excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission spectra of EA in PEG solvents were tested (Fig. 11a) and are presented in Table 10 . The emission wavelength for EA in both PEG200 and PEG300 was 403 nm, with a Stokes shift of 53 nm. These emission peaks were similar to those observed in alcoholic solvents and are likely due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the alcohol hydroxyl groups of the solvents and the EA molecules. As the molecular weight increased to 400, the emission wavelength red shifted to 520 nm, at which the Stokes shift increased to 170 nm, its fluorescence intensity decreased, QY decreased, and τ increased. Due to the super-viscosity effect caused by the high viscosity of PEGs, the molecules may become more constrained, leading to conformational changes. Additionally, the viscosity of the solvent can influence the rotational rate of the fluorescent molecules and increase the interaction between the molecules and the solvent. These factors collectively impact the energy level structure of the molecules' excited and fluorescent states. These changes affect, among other things, the fluorescence properties of the EA molecules. When the molecular weight is increased to 600, the emission peak at 520 nm decreased, and the emission peak at 403 nm abruptly increased. The semi-solid waxy state of PEG600, which has a freezing point of about 15–20 °C and is very easy to solidify in daily experiments, may be the cause of the double fluorescence phenomenon of the anti-Kasha rule. Its excessive viscosity rather limits the effect of the viscosity effect and causes ellagic acid to form, as previously mentioned, two different CT state conformations, which in turn causes the appearance of the double emission phenomenon. In addition, the fluorescence emission spectra of different concentrations of EA/PEG400 solutions at the optimum excitation wavelength are shown in Fig. 11b, which also showed the ACQ phenomenon, and the fluorescence intensity of EA showed a tendency to be increased and then decreased with the increase in concentration. The strongest fluorescence intensity was observed at 20 ppm.

Furthermore, we noted that the absorption spectra of EA/PEG solvents (Fig. 11d) exhibited similar absorption peaks to those of EA/alcohol solvents, primarily at 258 nm and 360 nm. The absorption peaks of EA/PEG400 experienced a notable alteration, wherein the absorption peaks at short wavelengths split into two distinct peaks at 254 nm and 280 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum DFT calculations yield of EA in organic solvents

ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we thoroughly examined the fluorescence properties of EA in various solvents. The findings showed that increasing the pH of the aqueous solution led to the deprotonation of the phenolic hydroxyl group in EA molecules. Consequently, the fluorescence emission peak shifted red towards longer wavelengths, coupled with an increase in fluorescence intensity. However, at a pH above 12, the emission peak of the EA solution reverted to approximately 440 nm, accompanied by a marked decline in fluorescence intensity and a decrease in the Stokes shift, due to the breakdown of the lactone structure within EA. Additionally, EA exhibited slight solubility in alcoholic solvents. In monohydric alcohol solvents, the fluorescence intensity of EA increased as the dielectric constant decreased. For polyol solvents, viscosity was the critical factor determining the fluorescence properties of EA. In contrast, nitrogenous solvents readily formed hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl protons of EA, altering the electron density distribution on the aromatic ring of EA and enhancing its electronic effects, which resulted in a red-shift of the emission peak. PEGs proved to be stable, healthy, and effective solvents for EA, favorably influencing its fluorescence emission properties. Moreover, EA molecules experienced fluorescence quenching in aggregated states due to strong intermolecular interactions, which limits their applicability in areas such as solid environment detection and microphase visualization in various solvents. This research provided fundamental and extensive information for the future fluorescent applications of EA.

Ziting Yuan: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Wei Tang: Resources, Methodology. Bin Sun: Investigation. Kai Li: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources. Li Yuan: Investigation. Yujin Zhang: Writing – review & editing.
